summary:
The trigeminal nerve plays a crucial indirect role in causing cardiac dysarrhythmias during tooth extraction due to potential vagal stimulation leading to altered autonomic function; specific examples involving glossopharyngeal neuralgia support this mechanism. Damage or irritation to related facial nerves may disrupt normal heart rate regulation secondary to systemic physiological changes triggered by dental interventions. To keep it concise while focusing only on essential information regarding causation,

The primary cause involves vagal stimulation from the trigeminal nerve which affects autonomic function, possibly triggering arrhythmias. Facial nerve injury during extraction could also induce cardiopulmonary complications altering usual heart activity patterns. Sentence count maintained at two without unnecessary elaborations.
